questions.question: NALSD's AdWords example assumes 500,000 search queries per second. For napkin-math capacity planning, this figure is primarily used to:
options: Size log ingestion, storage growth, and downstream join/index throughput
Determine the number of interviewers on a hiring committee
Calculate CDN cache TTL for static images only
Set the TCP window size on every client browser
questions.explanation: Transaction-rate assumptions drive log volume (~2 KB/query), disk IOPS, RAM for indexes, and worker counts—core NALSD practice of grounding design in quantitative load.

questions.question: In the AdWords CTR design, query logs are estimated at ~2 KB per entry and clicks are ~2% of query volume. Rough daily query-log storage is on the order of:
options: ~80+ TB per day (500K QPS × 2 KB × 86,400 s, rounded up aggressively)
~80 MB per day because clicks dominate storage
~8 GB per day because logs compress to zero
~800 PB per day requiring immediate quantum storage
questions.explanation: NALSD multiplies QPS × entry size × seconds/day (rounding up) to expose that single-machine storage is implausible—forcing distributed redesign in the feasibility phase.

questions.question: When estimating whether a dashboard can load in under one second, which resource check is most relevant for serving pre-aggregated CTR data from indexes in RAM?
options: Memory read latency and index lookup throughput—not cross-continent RTT on every row
Only cross-region network RTT for every byte
Tape backup restore time for cold archives
Git clone time for the application repository
questions.explanation: Fast dashboards depend on data locality in memory/SSD with proper indexes; napkin math separates in-process lookups from disk and WAN costs in the latency hierarchy.

questions.question: In NALSD's basic design phase, the question 'Is it possible?' means:
options: Ignoring hardware limits, what design satisfies requirements in principle (e.g., one machine with SQL indexes)?
Proving the design is already production-ready on current GCP quotas
Choosing the cheapest CDN provider before defining features
Eliminating all failure modes before writing any code
questions.explanation: NALSD starts unconstrained—can logic and algorithms meet functional needs?—before 'Can we do better?' optimizes complexity, then feasibility adds real-world limits.

questions.question: The NALSD question 'Is it feasible?' is asked after scaling requirements dramatically. It primarily tests whether:
options: The design can meet constraints of money, hardware, bandwidth, and disk IOPS—often requiring distribution
The team has memorized every Google internal RPC
Interviewers prefer whiteboard markers over digital tools
Strong consistency is impossible and must be abandoned always
questions.explanation: Feasibility applies napkin math: ~100 TB/day and 500K QPS break single-machine assumptions, pushing toward sharded stores, MapReduce/streaming, and realistic machine counts.

questions.question: When NALSD asks 'Is it resilient?', the recommended mental test is:
options: What happens when this component fails—and can we still meet SLOs or degrade gracefully?
Whether the design uses the newest programming language
If every server has identical CPU clock speed
Whether logs can be deleted to save disk immediately
questions.explanation: Resilience phase probes SPOFs (power, disk, network, datacenter) and failover—NALSD shows one machine fails SLOs on simple reboot; replication and reprocessing cover disasters.

questions.question: Which ordering correctly reflects the typical latency hierarchy emphasized in Google systems architecture prep?
options: Memory read < SSD read < rotating disk read < cross-region network RTT
Cross-region RTT < memory read < SSD read < disk read
Disk read < memory read < LAN RTT < SSD read
All four are within 1 µs at planetary scale
questions.explanation: Interview prep and NALSD stress orders-of-magnitude: RAM/near-data is fastest; mechanical disk slower; cross-continent RTT often dominates interactive paths if round-tripped per row.

questions.question: NALSD defines AdWords dashboard SLOs including '99.9% of the time, CTR data displayed is less than 5 minutes old.' This SLO primarily drives:
options: Streaming/join pipeline design instead of daily batch-only MapReduce
Elimination of all indexes on ad_id
Serving dashboards only from tape backups
Removing advertiser-facing APIs entirely
questions.explanation: Freshness SLOs bound end-to-end log join latency—batch MapReduce on huge windows misses 5-minute targets, motivating LogJoiner, QueryMap, ClickMap, and replication.

questions.question: In the AdWords CTR example, click logs and query logs are kept separate (joined on query_id) because:
options: Click URLs have size limits; query logs hold richer fields needed for many downstream metrics beyond CTR
SQL databases cannot perform joins
query_id is always NULL in production
Advertisers only care about impressions, not clicks
questions.explanation: NALSD acknowledges adding search_term to click logs might work in a toy scope, but at scale separate logs avoid URL size limits and support multiple analytics products from one query stream.

questions.question: An error budget tied to an SLO (e.g., 99.9% dashboard freshness) is used in design iterations to:
options: Quantify acceptable unreliability so teams balance feature velocity against resilience investments
Eliminate the need for monitoring and alerting
Guarantee zero failures forever once defined
Replace all napkin math with marketing slogans
questions.explanation: SRE book Ch. 4: error budgets convert SLOs into allowable failure—NALSD cites them when dual shard loss may briefly show stale CTR while reprocessing stays within budget.

questions.question: A Reddit candidate failed NALSD because the interviewer disengaged mid-interview. What is the most likely cause?
options: Skipped critical requirements or napkin math early — interviewer concluded the approach would not scale
Used too many colors on the whiteboard
Asked clarifying questions — Google penalizes all questions
Proposed replication — replication is never allowed in NALSD
questions.explanation: c3lrks comments: disengagement often means you missed a key clarifier, capacity estimate, or constraint. NALSD scores process; losing the interviewer early is a signal you went off-track.

questions.question: Reddit NALSD failure thread: you must back design choices with numbers. Which estimate is most often missing in failed interviews?
options: Machine/storage/bandwidth counts derived from QPS and retention — not hand-wavy 'we'll shard'
Exact hex color of the dashboard UI
Number of interviewers in the loop
Alphabetical sort order of service names
questions.explanation: c3lrks and o6ozcj stress napkin math. Saying 'scale horizontally' without sizing nodes, disks, or network transfer dooms NALSD credibility.

questions.question: You propose replicating 5 PB cross-region over a 10 Gbps link. A Reddit/HN theme in NALSD prep: why might an interviewer push back?
options: Transfer at 10 Gbps takes weeks/months — design must account for physics and recovery time objectives
5 PB is too small to matter at Google scale
10 Gbps exceeds all cloud provider limits
Replication is always instantaneous if you use SSDs
questions.explanation: Popular NALSD failure pattern: ignoring transfer time and bandwidth reality. Back-of-envelope: 5 PB at 10 Gbps ≈ 40+ days — unacceptable for many RTOs.

questions.question: In NALSD, jumping straight to a detailed component diagram without stating user-facing requirements first is weak because:
options: Interviewers cannot tell if the design solves the right problem or meets SLOs — requirements anchor all math
Google forbids diagrams in NALSD
Requirements are only for product managers, not SRE
SLOs are irrelevant to systems design
questions.explanation: Reddit failure stories align with official NALSD phases: clarify possible → quantify → resilient. Skipping requirements mirrors c3lrks 'interviewer already decided no-hire' pattern.

questions.question: Reddit comments note SRECon NALSD workshop examples feel 'trivial' vs real interviews. Best prep adjustment?
options: Practice with a partner on underspecified prompts — force your own clarifiers and full napkin math beyond workshop slides
Memorize workshop slides verbatim and repeat them
Skip quantitative sections — workshops don't use numbers
Only study abstract SWE system design blogs
questions.explanation: c3lrks: workshop helps framing but real NALSD goes deeper on calculations. Pair practice with different approaches is repeatedly recommended on Reddit.

questions.question: r/sre o6ozcj comments say NALSD is guaranteed in Google SRE loops. When iterating design, what order matches NALSD?
options: Possible → better → feasible (capacity) → resilient → better again
Resilient first, requirements never
Pick microservices immediately, then stop
Only optimize UI mockups
questions.explanation: Workbook NALSD phases and Reddit prep threads align: iterate through feasibility and resilience with numbers, not one-shot architecture theater.

questions.question: You estimate 100K writes/sec × 1 KB × 86400 sec/day for logs. Reddit NALSD failures often omit:
options: Retention multiplier — daily ingest × days retained = total storage (plus replication factor)
The need to multiply by zero
Converting bytes to bits incorrectly only
User's favorite programming language
questions.explanation: NALSD napkin math must chain: QPS → daily volume → retention window → replication. Missing retention is a common c3lrks-style gap.

questions.question: Reddit ExperiencedDevs NALSD confusion thread: when asked 'Is it resilient?', the strongest answer starts with:
options: Named failure modes (machine, rack, zone, region) and how SLO/error budget is preserved under each
Assertion that cloud providers never fail
Deleting all backups to save cost
Single-server design because it's simpler to explain
questions.explanation: NALSD resilience phase expects explicit failure analysis — Reddit confusion often comes from treating resilience as buzzword vs structured degradation/failover plan.
